findings.title: Composition: buy_logic/sell_logic silently degrade to OR for any non-AND string — validator gap is asymmetric with direction
evidence: Trigger: POST /api/backtest/quick with body { buy_logic: "ALL", sell_logic: "ALL", ... }.
Pydantic accepts the request. No validator exists for buy_logic or sell_logic; the PR only added validators for direction and ticker.
eval_rules() in signal_engine.py: return all(results) if logic == "AND" else any(results). Any string other than "AND" falls through to any(results) — OR semantics.
A user who sends buy_logic: "all" (lowercase), "BOTH", "&&", or a typo gets a silently wrong backtest (OR logic instead of AND) with HTTP 200 and no error indication.

The PR added a validator for direction (guarding "long"/"short") but did not add the analogous guard for buy_logic/sell_logic, making the validation surface asymmetric across fields with the same silent-fallback risk.
Same gap exists in BatchQuickBacktestRequest which also has buy_logic/sell_logic without validators.
Fix: add @field_validator("buy_logic", "sell_logic") enforcing v in ("AND", "OR") on both QuickBacktestRequest and BatchQuickBacktestRequest.

findings.title: Composition: batch backtest symbols bypass the new per-element ticker validator — invalid symbols produce HTTP 200 error rows instead of HTTP 422
evidence: Trigger: POST /api/backtest/quick/batch with body { symbols: ["", "AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA", "VALID"], ... }.

BatchQuickBacktestRequest has no field_validator on the symbols list. The validate_ticker validator that strips and upper-cases the ticker, and rejects empty/oversized values, only exists on QuickBacktestRequest.
The batch loop at line 210 constructs QuickBacktestRequest(ticker=symbol, ...) for each element. Pydantic fires validate_ticker during construction and raises ValueError for invalid symbols.
The broad except Exception at line 225 catches this: result = QuickBacktestResult(ticker=symbol.upper(), error=str(e)). The request returns HTTP 200 with per-symbol error rows.
Effect: callers cannot distinguish a validation failure from a legitimate backtest error (e.g. no data returned for a valid symbol). Both appear as error rows in the results array.
The PR intent (F28) was to harden input validation at the request boundary, but the batch path absorbs validation errors internally instead of rejecting them with 422.

findings.title: Cascade: all bots sharing a fetch_ohlcv_async Future receive simultaneous exceptions when _fetch fails, then retry-spike in the next tick window
evidence: Setup: N bot coroutines all call fetch_ohlcv_async for the same (symbol, start, end, interval, source, extended_hours) key on the same poll tick.
Coroutine A creates the executor Future, stores it in _async_ohlcv_futures[key], and enters await fut. Coroutines B..N each find existing not done and enter return await existing.
_fetch raises (e.g. provider HTTP 500 or network timeout). The Future becomes done with an exception.
All N coroutines are woken simultaneously with the exception. Each propagates it to bot_runner._tick() which logs WARN and returns. A fires finally: _async_ohlcv_futures.pop(key).

On the next poll tick, all N bots retry simultaneously. If the provider is still down, the same N-way spike occurs again. Unlike a staggered retry, all N executor threads attempt the failing call in the same narrow window.

Under normal load (2-5 bots per symbol) this is benign. Under a deployment with many bots on the same high-frequency symbol (e.g. SPY with 10+ bots), each tick window triggers a burst of N near-simultaneous provider calls when the provider is degraded — the opposite of backoff behavior.
This is an emergent property of the dedup design: dedup works well under success but amplifies synchronized failure.

residual_risks: _async_ohlcv_futures is an unbounded module-level dict. Entries are popped in finally blocks, so they cannot accumulate across completed calls. Under normal operation the dict is empty most of the time. But if a bot coroutine is cancelled (CancelledError) after joining a shared future as a secondary waiter, the finally in the primary coroutine (not the secondary) removes the key — the secondary gets the cancellation, not a dict leak. This is correct but worth confirming via code inspection if cancel behavior changes.

SetFundRequest.amount uses ge=0 (zero allowed) while UpdateBotRequest.allocated_capital uses gt=0 (strictly positive). This asymmetry means the fund can be zeroed via PUT /api/bots/fund, which switches the FundBar UI back to the initial-setup state (fund.bot_fund === 0 check). This is probably intentional as a reset mechanism but is undocumented.

The FundBar inputError state is local to the component and is not reset when the fund prop transitions from 0 to non-zero (i.e., after a successful onSetFund call the component re-renders from the zero-fund branch to the non-zero branch, naturally discarding state). No risk in practice, but the component does not call setInput("") after a successful set, so if onSetFund fails silently (no rejection propagated back to FundBar), the input field retains the submitted value with no error shown.
testing_gaps: No test constructs a QuickBacktestRequest with buy_logic="NAND" and verifies HTTP 422 rather than a silently OR-logic result.
No test sends a BatchQuickBacktestRequest with an empty string in the symbols array and verifies whether the response is 422 (desired per F28 intent) or 200 with an error row (current behavior).
No concurrent test exercises two coroutines sharing a fetch_ohlcv_async Future where _fetch raises, to confirm both callers receive the exception rather than one hanging or the dict leaking.
